title: There's a planet made entirely of diamond — twice the size of Earth.
short: 55 Cancri e, about 40 light-years away, has a carbon-rich interior that formed into diamond under extreme pressure. Its estimated value: 26.9 nonillion dollars.
twist: The universe literally made a planet out of bling. We can't afford it.
more: 55 Cancri e orbits its star in just 18 hours and has surface temperatures around 2,400°C. At roughly twice Earth's diameter and eight times its mass, the amount of diamond is staggering. Of course, mining it is slightly impractical — it's 40 light-years away and the surface is molten lava. But it's nice to know it's out there.

title: Crows can recognize your face — and hold grudges for years.
short: Researchers who captured and tagged crows wore specific masks. Years later, crows still scolded and dive-bombed anyone wearing those masks — even people the crows had never met.
twist: They remember what you did. And they told their friends.
more: The University of Washington conducted a landmark study where researchers wore caveman masks while trapping crows. Years later, crows on campus still mobbed anyone wearing that mask — even new crows that weren't alive during the original capture. This means crows teach their offspring which humans are dangerous. They have a social 'most wanted' list.

title: There are more dead people on Facebook than living users in some countries.
short: Over 30 million Facebook accounts belong to deceased users. The platform gains about 8,000 dead accounts per day. By 2070, dead users may outnumber living ones.
twist: The largest graveyard in history is a website.
more: A 2019 Oxford study projected that by 2070, Facebook's dead users could outnumber living ones. Facebook has introduced 'memorialized' accounts to manage this. What happens to your digital identity after death? For the first time in history, the dead can still 'speak' through their archived posts.

title: The coldest temperature ever recorded in the universe was created on Earth.
short: Scientists at MIT cooled sodium atoms to 500 nanokelvin — half a billionth of a degree above absolute zero. That's colder than anything known to exist naturally in space.
twist: We out-froze the void of space. In a lab. On purpose.
more: The coldest natural temperature in the universe is the cosmic microwave background radiation at about 2.7 kelvin. But in laboratories, scientists have achieved temperatures millions of times colder. At these extremes, atoms form a Bose-Einstein condensate — a state of matter where particles lose individual identity and act as a single quantum entity.
